steps.title: Open the appositive
steps.content: The blank comes after was a and must introduce the noun supernova plus punctuation before the phrase that defines it: the explosion of a massive star. That defining phrase is an appositive because it renames and explains supernova. Since the sentence already has a dash after the definition, before the clause about American astronomer Josef Johnson, the blank needs punctuation that opens the same dash pair.

steps.title: Match the dash pair
steps.content: Choice A uses a comma before the definition, but the sentence later closes the definition with a dash, creating mismatched punctuation. Choice B uses a period, which splits off the defining phrase and leaves the following material as a fragment. Choice D uses a colon, but the later dash makes that an awkward mixed frame for a mid-sentence appositive. The remaining choice opens the defining phrase with a dash, matching the later dash and correctly setting off the appositive. The answer is C.
